retrieved_docs.title: Dental Mini-Implants -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Therefore, the current evidence must be reviewed and synthesized with the available clinical data on the survival of mini implants for definitive prosthodontic treatment. Following factors influence the clinical success of mini implants: 1. Mini implant design: Improvement of implant shape, thread patterns and surface treatments lead to enhanced primary stability and faster osseointegration [24] [25] 2. Size of mini-implant effects the area of possible retention in bones [1] 3. Favorable occlusion and masticatory forces significantly influence mini-implant success [26] [27] 4. Number and position of mini-implant within the prosthesis affect the forces acting on the bone surrounding the implants [26] [27] 5. The stress distribution is influenced by implant diameter and shape along with the load direction [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Ankyloglossia (Tongue-Tie) -- Introduction -- Definition
retrieved_docs.content: No standard definition of ankyloglossia has been agreed on. The International Affiliation of Tongue-Tie Professionals defines the lingual frenulum as a tissue remnant located in the midline between the tongue's ventral surface and the mouth's floor. [2] When the lingual frenulum limits the function of the tongue, it is called symptomatic tongue-tie or symptomatic ankyloglossia. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: Class I malocclusion is further categorized into three types by Dewey. In Dewey type 1, the incisors are crowded, the canines are positioned labially, or both. [5] In Dewey type 2, the maxillary incisors protrude. In type 3, the anterior teeth occlude edge to edge, or there is a crossbite or both. [5] The Anderson classification further categorizes class I malocclusion into types 4 and 5. In type 4, there is a posterior crossbite, which could be unilateral or bilateral. Type 5 occlusion is a class I molar relationship with mesioversion of the permanent first molar due to the extraction of a second deciduous molar or premolar. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Ankyloglossia (Tongue-Tie) -- Introduction -- Definition
retrieved_docs.content: In 2020, a group of otolaryngologists with expertise in managing tongue-tie formulated a clinical consensus statement. They agreed on a definition of tongue-tie as a "condition of limited tongue mobility caused by a restrictive lingual frenulum." [1]  This panel of experts also acknowledged that professionals have been using the terms anterior and posterior ankyloglossia in recent years. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: A class II division 2 is when the maxillary central incisors are palatally inclined and may be overlapped by the maxillary lateral incisors. A deep overbite and a broad maxillary arch define a class II division 2. There is a normal upper lip seal and a deep mental groove. Unlike division 1, division 2 has a normal-sized mandible. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The tetragonal phase can be retained at room temperature by the addition of 3 mol% yttria. Yttria-stabilized tetragonal zirconia polycrystal (Y-TZP) provides mechanical properties superior to other all-ceramic systems due to the tetragonal-to-monoclinic phase transformation. Thus, Y-TZP has been increasingly used as a core material for all-ceramic restorations. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: There are many ways to examine the quality of occlusion, and this is exemplified when considering dental implants or denture design. In maxillofacial trauma, occlusion can be altered from the trauma itself or suboptimal management of fractures. Keeping the dental occlusion as the foundation for these repairs prevents long-term malocclusion. [3] In other cases, malocclusion can be developmental and adversely impact cosmesis, which is the case for some patients seeking orthognathic surgery. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Ankyloglossia (Tongue-Tie) -- Introduction -- Definition
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior ankyloglossia is classic ankyloglossia, where the frenulum attaches at or close to the tongue tip, limiting tongue mobility. [2] Notably, 'anterior ankyloglossia' and 'ankyloglossia' are interchangeable. [1] Diagnosis of posterior ankyloglossia remains a subject of controversy. Posterior ankyloglossia occurs when the frenulum attaches to the posterior aspect of the ventral surface of the tongue and restricts tongue mobility. [1] Some also use the term to refer to the submucosal tethering of the tongue. [1] Others consider posterior ankyloglossia normal frenulum attachment and attribute breastfeeding difficulties to other factors, like nipple anatomy, maternal milk production, and maternal experience. [2] Maxillary lip frenulum tethering has also been reported to affect breastfeeding. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Bruxism Management -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Intermittent bruxism is prevalent, particularly among children. Often, patients remain asymptomatic, and treatment is not warranted. [17] Typically, clinicians treat bruxism when adverse effects on oral structures occur or in the presence of concurrent conditions such as neurological or sleep disorders or those listed below. Mechanical wear of the teeth causing the loss of occlusal morphology and flattening of the occlusal surfaces Hypersensitive teeth Fractured teeth Fractures of restorations, crowns, and partial prosthesis Failure of dental implants Masticatory muscle hypertrophy Tenderness and stiffness in jaw muscles Restricted opening of the mouth Temporomandibular joint pain Preauricular pain Clicking and tenderness of the temporomandibular joint Headaches due to temporalis muscle tightness and tenderness Unpleasant loud noises during sleep that cause sleep disturbances [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Various classification methods for dental carious lesions have been developed, each with advantages and limitations. Newer classification systems, like the International Caries Detection and Assessment System (ICDAS) and the American Dental Association Caries Classification System (ADA CCS), have evolved from the traditional Black classification to include the current understanding of the carious process, maximize healthy tooth surfaces, and prevent extension of the carious lesion.

retrieved_docs.content: The primary disadvantages of mini implants for definitive prosthodontic treatment are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As previously mentioned, teeth play a functional role in speech, with the loss of these teeth affecting the formation of the fricative sound and, by extension, many other sounds. Another important concept is the vertical dimension of occlusion. A lack of teeth, or extreme wear and destruction of teeth, can cause the jaw to overclose, resulting in accentuated nasolabial folds and an aged appearance, which speaks to the cosmetic function of teeth. As the soft tissues of the face lose support, phonation, bolus creation, and swallowing can also be affected. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The term buccal crossbite refers to the buccal cusps of the lower teeth, which are occluding buccal to the buccal cusps of the upper teeth. Scissor bite refers to the condition when the buccal cusps of the lower teeth occlude lingual to the lingual cusps of the upper teeth. Crossbite malocclusion can have a skeletal or dental component or a combination.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to facial problems, orthodontics looks to improve the relationship between the maxillary and mandibular teeth when they come together and function. The biological and functional coordination of how teeth come together is termed occlusion. Over the years, a consensus has been reached on what features are regarded as an ideal or normal occlusion. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The following qualities define an ideal occlusion: class I molar and canine classification, 1- to 2-mm overjet, 1- to 2-mm overbite, slight to no crowding, and protective guidance movements. Furthermore, the incisal edges and buccal cusps of the teeth in the mandibular arch should align to form a smooth curve.

retrieved_docs.title: Bruxism Management -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Possible bruxism: The diagnosis is possible when based on self-reporting only. Probable bruxism: The diagnosis is probable based on self-reporting and clinical examination. Definite bruxism: The diagnosis is definite when clinicians incorporate self-reporting, clinical inspection, and polysomnographic recording, ideally accompanied by audio and video recordings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: adverse‑event reporting and clinical management. Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, the designation “Allowed qualifier” is applied within the scope of enzymology to the conditions قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), intestinal infection, and اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder). This indicates that enzyme‑related criteria or measurements are permissible as qualifiers when evaluating these gastrointestinal and oral disorders, thereby supporting more precise diagnostic assessment and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 5 -- Community 2956
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an “Allowed qualifier” is linked through the relationship “enzymologie” to three specific clinical conditions: اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), فالج (paralysis), and قرحات الطرفين السفليين (ulcers of the lower limbs). This indicates that enzymological considerations are recognized as permissible qualifiers when evaluating these disorders, suggesting that enzyme‑related mechanisms or assessments may be relevant in the diagnosis, pathophysiology, or management of tongue disturbances, paralysis, and lower‑extremity ulcerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 6 -- Community 2951
retrieved_docs.content: The designation “Allowed qualifier” is recognized as an appropriate term to use in the context of ošetřování (medical care) for patients with فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد (iron‑deficiency anemia) and also for patients with اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), indicating that this qualifier is permissible when providing care for both of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 5 -- Community 2948
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as both a complication and a target of care for several distinct medical conditions. It is linked to the complication of iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد) and also requires appropriate management for this anemia. In addition, adhesion (الالتصاق) is noted as a complication associated with allowed qualifier and is likewise an area that demands specific therapeutic attention. Tongue disorder (اِضطِراب اللسان) is another complication connected to allowed qualifier, and appropriate care measures are indicated for this condition. Finally, mitral valve stenosis (تضيُّق الصمام المترالي) is listed as a complication of allowed qualifier, with corresponding treatment considerations required to address this cardiac narrowing. Together, these relationships emphasize that allowed qualifier encompasses a range of complications—iron‑deficiency anemia, adhesion, tongue disorder, and mitral valve stenosis—and that each of these conditions necessitates dedicated clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 5 -- Community 2961
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “Allowed qualifier” is linked by the relationship “komplikace” to two distinct conditions: “انسداد الحالب” (ureteral obstruction) and “اِضطِراب اللسان” (tongue disorder). In this context, “Allowed qualifier” is identified as a complication of both ureteral obstruction and tongue disorder, highlighting the need for clinicians to consider this qualifier when evaluating patients with either of these conditions. Recognizing such complications can inform diagnostic assessment and guide appropriate management strategies for affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is a form of elevated arterial pressure that is identified and interpreted through measurement of ضغط الدم (blood pressure). The concept of ضغط الدم includes the entire spectrum of arterial pressures and is directly related to ضغط الدم المنخفض (low blood pressure), illustrating the continuum from hypotension to hypertension. Recognizing how essential hypertension is defined by ضغط الدم and how ضغط الدم connects with ضغط الدم المنخفض helps clinicians assess, diagnose, and manage blood pressure disorders more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2819
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ) is closely related and may be considered synonymous with coronary artery occlusion (انسداد الشريان التاجي). It is also mapped to coronary artery embolism (انصمام الشريان التاجي), indicating that these terms describe similar pathological events in which a blockage of a coronary artery—whether by occlusion or embolic material—leads to sudden injury of the heart muscle. Recognizing the interchangeability of these concepts assists clinicians in diagnosing and managing the acute coronary syndrome that results from compromised coronary blood flow.
